FOR SAFETYREASONS. Subject: NSI Daily Market Report 11/11/2025

Hi Deepesh

The market is flat this morning and looking before direction.

Crude oil prices have opened lower this morning after Lukoil had declared

force majeure on its operations at the West Qurna-2 field, one of Iraq's

biggest oil fields, due to the US sanctions on the two Russian oil majors.

The West Qurna-2 field produces around 400,000 barrels of crude a day, which

Lukoil had 75% in the field. Following the US sanctions on the 22nd of

October, buyers affiliated with Russian oil have gradually dropped off.

Several Indian refiners have turned to the Middle East and the America's for

energy supply - Trump has commented that India has "stopped doing the

Russian oil - it's been reduced very substantially," in addition to the US

being close to a trade deal with India. Chinese refiners have claimed that

they are also turning away from Russian oil, but it was noted that Chinese

independent refiners will likely continue purchases of Russian energy

products, on top of Iraq stopping all cash and crude payments to Lukoil.

Last week, it was reported that Iraq's state oil marketing company SOMO had

cancelled three crude loading from Lukoil which were scheduled for the 11th,

18th, and 26th, making it apparent that Iraq doesn't want to handle to

now-sanctioned barrels and not wanting to risk any back-lash from the US.

Lukoil has been left stranded with no immediate deal to sell its

international assets after Gunvor, a Switzerland-based commodity trader, had

opted out after the US Treasury had called the group a Russian "puppet" in

its statement saying that "President Trump has been clear that the war must

end immediately. As long as Putin continues the senseless killings, the

Kremlin's puppet, Gunvor, will never get a license to operate and profit".

With no option to sell its assets, including the West Qurna-2 that's one of

the biggest, Lukoil's near-term production and supply from the Iraqi oil

field becomes increasingly uncertain. Generally, this news would push prices

higher, however its effect on prices have been supressed because oversupply

projections have been the dominant sentiment on oil markets. "As OPEC

production increases grind on, global oil balances are acquiring an

increasingly bearish hue on the supply side of the ledger with demand still

trending lower in conjunction with a slowed economic growth path among major

oil-consuming countries," commented Ritterbusch and Associates, an outlet

that shares the sentiment. Currently, traders are keeping a close eye for

OPEC's latest monthly report which is scheduled to be released tomorrow

(Wednesday), along with the EIA's monthly report; both groups have

conflicting opinions of the market and are likely to stick with their demand

and supply projections. Even though OPEC+ has reduced its output hike for

December and has decided to make no increases for the first few months of

2026 - based on demand being low at the start of any year - as a whole, the

group has pushed that demand remains robust enough to support the increases

in supply, viewing the market as relatively balanced. On the other hand, the

International Energy Agency has warned that the markets are heading into a

significant supply overhang amid weakening demand, expecting that there'll

be a global glut in early 2026.

Russian crude oil deliveries to Asia has fallen by 4.2% from levels in 2024,

the lack of increases unexpected during the 2025 season. The Northern Sea

Route is largely used between Russia's oil fields and Asian buyers since it

is seen as a cheaper channel die the journey through it is ten days shorter

than the route via the Suez Canal; however, the route can only be used

during warmer months. In 2024, shipments via the route stood at 1.91 million

tons of crude, roughly equal to 14 million barrels, which the volumes

represent a 30% increase on the previous year. This year, Russian oil

exporters shipped 1.83 million tons of crude, equal to around 13.41 million

barrels via the Northern Sea Route, most of the delvieries went to China,

and a small portion going to South Korea - according to data reported by

Kommersant daily. For 2025, it was expected that deliveries would continue

to increase, but due to the mounting Western sanctions and prominent ice,

shipments via the Northern Sea Route is predicted to only account for just

1% of all exports, as well as a fraction of total Russian oil exports in

2026.

Nigeria's President Bola Tinubu has been enacting reforms to stimulate more

investments into the industry, which has pushed the country's production

average between 1.7 million barrels a day to 1.83 million barrels daily.

Over the next two years, Nigeria's national oil company is set to increase

oil production to 2 million barrels a day, according to its executive vice

president for upstream Udy Ntia, adding that it will be pumping 3 million

barrels daily by 2030. Currently, Nigeria is also considering to tap 1.7

billion barrels in new oil supply, on top of 7.7 trillion cu ft of natural

gas, after it receives approval for plans with the 43 field development this

year, which involves committing to around $20 billion in investments -

reported the chief executive of the Nigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory

Commission, Gbenga Komolafe. The executive also reported that three final

investment decisions for the Bonga North, Ubeta Gas, and HI Gas Project have

been made this year, which will allow them to tap into around 2 trillion cu

ft of new natural gas supply. The breakdown of the FIDs are that $5 billion

is for Shell's Bonga North, $2 billion is for the HI Gas Project (also run

by Shell), and $500 million for TotalEnergies' Ubeta Gas project - the FID's

totalling to $7.5 billion. Although Nigeria is making strong efforts to

build up its energy security and strengthen its position on energy markets,

the nation continues to run into drawbacks that's continuously pushed them

behind. Recently, an audit commissioned by Nigeria's parliament had found

that the state lost around $300 billion to oil theft and pipeline vandalism,

findings are interim and will potentially end up being much higher. The two

main factors for Nigeria's oil production decline are pipeline vandalism and

oil theft, discouraging any additional investments necessary for reversing

production trends and boosting trade. Additionally, Big Oil's strategy has

manipulated their position in Nigeria, favouring other locations with better

prospects. Considering these ongoing problems, the Nigerian government is

trying to reverse these trends with legislative reforms and cracking down on

prosecutions of pipeline theft and vandalism.

IT IS EXPLICITLY UNDERSTOOD THAT, NOTWITHSTANDING ANYTHING TO THE CONTRARY HEREIN, NO SPEED WARRANTY APPLIES TO CONDITIONS EXCEEDING B4 AND DSS 3, CONTRARY CURRENTS, SEABUOY TO SEABUOY ON VOYAGES LESS THAN 48 HOURS AND/OR 24 HRS AFTER COSP/PRIOR EOSP, EXCLUDING CANALS, NARROW/RESTRICTED/TIDAL AFFECTED WATERS AND DUE TO REASONS OF SAFETY, OR POOR VISIBILITY ETC, PERIODS WHEN THE SPEED IS REDUCED DUE TO CHRTRS' INSTRUCTIONS. FOR THE PURPOSE OF EVALUATING VESSELS PERFORMANCE UNDER THIS CHARTER PARTY IT IS AGREED THAT ALL DAYS WHERE THE WEATHER EXCEEDS BEAUFORT FORCE 4 AND/OR DOUGLAS SEA STATE 3 (WAVE/SWELL/SIGN HEIGHT UP TO 1.25MTRS), DAYS OF NEGATIVE CURRENT, 24 HRS AFTER COMMENCEMENT AND BEFORE ENDING OF SEA PASAGE OR RESTRICTED/OBSTRUCTED NAVIGATION AREAS ARE EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED FROM THE EVALUATION. POSITIVE CURRENT SHALL NOT BE APPLIED IN DEMINISHING VSLS SPEED PERFORMANCE.

THE SPEED SO DESCRIBED IS ON AN AVERAGE BASIS THE ENTIRE CHARTER PERIOD, INCLUDING BALLAST VOYAGES, 24HR DAILY EVALUATION, PROVIDED NO BOTTOM FOULING DUE TO IMMOBILIZATION FOR 25 DAYS COMBINED FOLLOWING VESSEL DELIVERY TO CHARTER (PORT(S) OR ANCHORAGE(S) OR SHIFTING(S) OF LESS THAN 24HRS TO BE EXCLUDED).

VESSEL HAS THE LIBERTY OF STEAMING AT SLOW SPEED OR STOP/BEING IDLE FOR THE PURPOSES OF BALLAST EXCHANGE IF REQUIRED AT CHARTERERS TIME/COST. COST OF BALLAST TREATMENT/EXCHANGE IF REQUIRED AND ANY LOSS OF SPEED/TIME TO BE FOR CHRTRS A/C.

WORD 'ABT' STANDS FOR +/- 0.5 KNOT ON SPEED AND +/-5% ON IFO/MDO CONSUMPTIONS.

VESSEL CONSUMES ADDITIONAL IFO WHILE PERFORMING BALLAST EXCHANGE OPERATIONS AND/OR CLEANING OF VESSEL'S HOLDS.

VSL CONSUMES LSMGO FOR GENERATORS AND MAIN ENGINE IN CONFINED/NARROW WATERS-CHANNELS, SHALLOW OR CONGESTED WATERS, CANALS,STRAITS, RIVERS, WHEN SAILING IN BAD WEATHER/ROUGH SEA OR BELOW 10 KNOTS, ENTERING LEAVING PORTS AND IN GENERAL RESTRICTED TO NAVIGATION/MANOEUVRING AREA WITH OR WITHOUT PILOTS, TO MASTER'S DISCRETION.AS WELL AS, FOR STARTING AND STOPPING GENERATORS AND BOILER.

VESSEL NOT FITTED WITH SCRUBBERS, CHARTERERS TO SUPPLY BUNKERS AS PER LATEST REGULATIONS IN FORCE AND M/E MAKER DESIGN AND PARAMETERS.

Owners are delivering the vessel to Charterers with VLSFO (max 0.50% sulfur) and LSMGO (max 0.10% sulfur). During the currency of this charter, Charterers are only permitted to supply these grades to the vessel.

AGW, sub vessel is expected to berth on 04-05/12 subject to sailing of

earlier arrived vessels, terminal confirmation & as per tide .

NOTE: As per norms discharge rate 328 MT per hour to be maintain for LAB to

avoid additional berth hire charges.

Vessel seniority for berthing consider as per vessel arrival and submitting

all the documents including cargo discharge instruction before port berthing

meeting i.e 1000 hrs LT

NOTE: : PLEASE NOTE THIS ABOVE BERTHING LINE UP IS TENTATIVE & SUBJECT TO

CHANGE AS PER ARRIVAL OF THE VESSEL, DRAFT, SPEED AND AVAILIBILITY OF BERTH

/ PILOT.

The above is for your kind information and please be guided accordingly.

Please note that HD-5,6 and HD-07 are at inside docks area and all movements

will be during high tide only.

Kindly note during one tide only 3 vessel can come in and 2 vessel can go

out of the docks area, so even if the vessel is ready to sail out, sailing

will be as per the seniority for sailing readiness, therefore sailing out

from docks is subject to availability of sailing slot as per the sailing

turn, it is common that the vessel are waiting around 2-3 days at inside

docks due to non availability of sailing slot.

In Case of Severe congestion Port might opt for vessel to undergo cargo

operation double banking Mode, Please be noted that if vessel completes the

discharge operation and sailing slot is not available the port might opt for

double banking operation of your good vessel with another vessel till the

sailing slot is available.

ABOUT ON SPEED AND CONSUMPTION CLARIFIED AS MEANING 5% ALLOWANCE ON CONSUMPTION AND 0.5 KNOTS ON SPEED, BOTH IN OWNERS FAVOR. IT IS UNDERSTOOD THAT VESSEL NOT TO BE ORDERED TO SAIL ON ECONOMICAL SPEED/CONSUMPTION IN PIRACY RISKS AREAS

ALL DETAILS ABOUT AND GIVEN IN GOOD FAITH ANY SAVING IN CONSUMPTION MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY SPEED UNDERPERFORMANCE, ANY SAVING IN TIME MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY EXCESS CONSUMPTION, ANY SAVING IN FO MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST INCREASED MDO AND VICE VERSA, AND ANY OVERALL SAVING ON INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S) MUST BE SET OFF AGAINST AN OVERALL LOSS ON OTHER INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S). OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO A CREDIT FOR ANY BUNKER SAVINGS, WHICH ARE TO BE CALCULATED AS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN MAXIMUM QUANTITIES OF BUNKERS ALLOWED TO CONSUME AS PER ABOVE AND THE QUANTITY OF BUNKERS ACTUALLY CONSUMED DURING THE VOYAGE. IN THE EVENT THAT THERE IS A SPEED CLAIM, THE CREDIT FOR BUNKER SAVINGS SHALL BE SET-OFF AGAINST ANY LOSS OF TIME TO REDUCE OR EXTINGUISH SPEED CLAIM, AND IN THE EVENT BUNKERS SAVINGS ARE OF VALUE GREATER THAN SPEED CLAIM, OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO CREDIT FOR REMAINING BALANCE OF BUNKER SAVINGS AFTER SETTING OFF SPEED CLAIM.OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO A CREDIT FOR ANY TIME SAVED, WHICH IS TO BE CALCULATED AS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN MAXIMUM TIME THE SHIP WAS ALLOWED TO COMPLETE THE VOYAGE AS PER ABOVE AND THE TIME ACTUALLY TAKEN TO COMPLETE THE VOYAGE.OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO THE SAID CREDIT FOR TIME SAVED IRRESPECTIVE OF WHETHER THERE IS ANY OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM. IN THE EVENT THAT THERE IS AN OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, THE CREDIT FOR TIME SAVED SHALL BE SET-OFF AGAINST ANY OVER-CONSUMPTION TO REDUCE OR EXTINGUISH OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, AND IN THE EVENT TIME SAVED IS OF VALUE GREATER THAN OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO CREDIT FOR REMAINING BALANCE OF TIME SAVED AFTER SETTING OFF OVER- CONSUMPTION CLAIM

The vessel has 4 remote control grabs as described and as onboard. The grabs are accompanied by the manufacturer's certificate only and not by any other Class

attestation which would incorporate them into vessel's Cargo Gear system. Charterers may make use of the Grabs free of charge, but at their own risk and expenses being always responsible for the good condition of same which to be reported by testing prior use. Charterers are to remain fully responsible for any possible failure, and/or damage, and/or non-compatibility with the nature of the cargo to be lifted, and/or non compliance with the customary work in ports, and/or any other failure of whatsoever nature, Owners not to be responsible howsoever and vessel to remain on-hire and/or laytime is to keep counting.

SPEED/ CONS BALLAST/ LADEN:

Basis in good weather conditions not exceeding Beaufort scale 4, no adverse current,

and Douglas sea state 3 (which to be understood as max. 1.25m significant wave height for combined seas including wind and swell waves), always about:

-ballast/laden abt12.5kn/abt12kn on 24mt ABT (IFO 380 cst)

-port consumption: ABT 3 mt idle/ 6 ABT mt working (IFO 380 cst) without bunker heating

-port consumption in low temperatures +2 ABT mt (IFO 380 cst)

-ballasting/deballasting: ABT 3 mt per day pro rata (IFO 380 cst)

No extrapolations to be made for weather/sea conditions other than those described above.

Underperformance claim is to be restricted to good weather only and not extrapolated to the whole voyage.

Vessel's speed/ consumption warranted only during good weather days of 24 consecutive hours running between Master's reported positions noon to noon ( each a "GWD" ) Vessel may burn mdo during dead slow or slow steaming.

About ("ABT") is understood to mean 5% downwards in the speed and 5% upwards in the consumption.

Given speed and consumption basis no bottom fouling due to cumulative immobilization for extended period(s) in port(s) or anchorage(s) under this cp.

Favorable currents are not to be applied to reduce the calculated performance speed on good weather days, good weather performance speed to be ship's actual speed over the ground on good weather days. No m/e performance (mcr,rmp, etc,.) table to be provided.

The speed / consumption so described is on average basis taken over good weather days over the entire charter period, including ballast voyages, excluding periods during which reduction of speed for safety, congestion, narrow/restricted water and/or river-canal passage and/or reduced visibility and/or sea passages from safe berth to safe berth up to 24 hrs after/ prior cosp/eosp and/or periods when speed is reduced due to charterers or pilots orders.

Weather routing service, if employed, to be notified of the applicability of the word ''about '' to both the consumptions and speed, applying a 5 % upwards in the consumption and 5% downwards in the speed.

Present fixture to be performed only with the speed and consumption as described in vessel's description and Owners have the right to reject Charterer's request for any other different speed, even if same is in WOG condition regarding vessel's consumption.

Owners are entitled to a credit for any bunker savings, which are to be calculated as the difference between maximum quantities of bunkers the ship was allowed to consume as per above and the quantity of bunkers calculated by taking the average bunkers consumed during good weather days during the voyage extrapolated over the complete voyage.

Owners are entitled to the said credit for bunker savings irrespective of whether there is any speed claim. In the event that there is a speed claim, the credit for bunker savings shall be set-off against any loss of time to reduce or extinguish speed claim, and in the event bunkers savings are of value greater than speed claim, owners are entitled to credit for remaining balance of bunker savings after setting off speed claim.

Owners are entitled to a credit for any time saved, which is to be calculated as the difference between maximum time the ship was allowed to complete the voyage as per above and the time the time calculated by taking the average speed during good weather days during the voyage extrapolated over the complete voyage.

Owners are entitled to the said credit for time saved irrespective of whether there is any over-consumption claim. In the event that there is an over-consumption claim, the credit for time saved shall be set-off against any over-consumption to reduce or extinguish over-consumption claim, and in the event time saved is of value greater than over-consumption claim, owners are entitled to credit for remaining balance of time saved after setting off over-consumption claim.

In the event that there are no good weather days, owners shall be entitled to calculate bunker savings and/or time saved for purposes of said credits as the difference between actual speed and/or consumption in all weathers during the voyage and maximum time the ship was allowed to complete the voyage and/or maximum quantities of bunkers the ship was allowed to consume during the voyage.

FUEL OIL SPECIFICATION / TREATMENT:

The Charterers shall supply only suitable fuels as per specification to enable main propulsion and auxiliary machinery as well as boiler to operate efficiently and without harmful effects.

Fuels to be mineral oil based products of stable and homogeneous nature and shall comply, be delivered and sampled in accordance with international, regional or national regulations and latest CIMAC recommendations and ISO standard 8217:2017, ISO/PAS 23263:2019 and MARPOL Annex VI (or any subsequent amendments of the above); and shall not contain waste lubricants, tar oil, inorganic acids, chemicals or any other harmful substances.

When the vessel is ordered to trade within emission control zones (ECA), it is understood that the vessel's engines to burn DMZ ultra-low sulphur MGO or ULSHFO of max 0,1% sulphur content, else as per ISO 8217:2017; charterers to supply bunkers in compliance to fuel sulphur contents requirements. If charterers intend to supply biofuels, they must comply with ISO 8217:2024 specifications.

Comingling of ULSMGO supplies only is allowed.

Change-over procedures from HS fuel to ULS fuel will depend to ULS fuel's sulphur content and in any case not to be less than 2 hours prior entering an ECA.

Charterers have no right whatsoever to claim for loss of speed and bunkers over-consumption during the change-over period.

Bimco Fueleu maritime clause for TC parties 2024 to apply.

The Charterers shall be responsible to purchase and transfer into the Owners' nominated Emission Scheme account the Emission Allowances (EUA) corresponding to the Vessel's emissions under the scope of the applicable EU-ETS (European Union Emissions Trading System ) following the EU Directive EU 2023/959 - EU 2003/87 EC throughout the Charter Party period even when vessel has been delivered at sea (i.e passing) or at non eu port but with last port of cargo operation being eu port.

cii operations clause for TC parties 2022 to apply.

Bunker sampling as per ISO 13739:2020

BALLASTING:

For the safe performance of the vessel, when in long ballast voyage to cross oceans, it is master's absolute discretion to heavily ballast the ship, including flooding of No 3 hold. Ballasting/de-ballasting time, if any, as well as any drying-up time, to be for Charterers' account. Ballasting/ de-ballasting of hold no 3 to be effected / executed in a safe port or safe/sheltered anchorage, not en route during sailing. It is not allowed to partially ballast cargo hold no 3 for any reason. Ballasting/de-ballasting of hold No 3 is not to take place for reasons other than the above.

Vessel has the liberty of steaming at slow speed or stop/being idle for the purposes of ballast exchange, if required. In case that the water Salinity during ballast operations is less than 0.9PSU(1050 S/cm)Limit for BWTS Operation, ,vessel has the liberty to Ballast with untreated ballast and to Replace same at open sea basis IMO and local regulations or to carry her APT full with open sea salt water in order to use it for increase salinity and treat her ballast while at port with low salinity . Cost of discharging untreated ballast if required and any loss of speed /time to be for charterers account

The vessel has 4 remote control grabs as described and as onboard. The grabs

are accompanied by the manufacturer's certificate only and not by any other

Class

attestation which would incorporate them into vessel's Cargo Gear system.

Charterers may make use of the Grabs free of charge, but at their own risk

and expenses being always responsible for the good condition of same which

to be reported by testing prior use. Charterers are to remain fully

responsible for any possible failure, and/or damage, and/or

non-compatibility with the nature of the cargo to be lifted, and/or non

compliance with the customary work in ports, and/or any other failure of

whatsoever nature, Owners not to be responsible howsoever and vessel to

remain on-hire and/or laytime is to keep counting.

SPEED/ CONS BALLAST/ LADEN:

Basis in good weather conditions not exceeding Beaufort scale 4, no adverse

current,

and Douglas sea state 3 (which to be understood as max. 1.25m significant

wave height for combined seas including wind and swell waves), always about:

-ballast/laden abt12.5kn/abt12kn on 24mt ABT (IFO 380 cst)

-port consumption: ABT 3 mt idle/ 6 ABT mt working (IFO 380 cst) without

bunker heating

-port consumption in low temperatures +2 ABT mt (IFO 380 cst)

-ballasting/deballasting: ABT 3 mt per day pro rata (IFO 380 cst)

No extrapolations to be made for weather/sea conditions other than those

described above.

Underperformance claim is to be restricted to good weather only and not

extrapolated to the whole voyage.

Vessel's speed/ consumption warranted only during good weather days of 24

consecutive hours running between Master's reported positions noon to noon (

each a "GWD" ) Vessel may burn mdo during dead slow or slow steaming.

About ("ABT") is understood to mean 5% downwards in the speed and 5% upwards

in the consumption.

Given speed and consumption basis no bottom fouling due to cumulative

immobilization for extended period(s) in port(s) or anchorage(s) under this

cp.

Favorable currents are not to be applied to reduce the calculated

performance speed on good weather days, good weather performance speed to be

ship's actual speed over the ground on good weather days. No m/e performance

(mcr,rmp, etc,.) table to be provided.

The speed / consumption so described is on average basis taken over good

weather days over the entire charter period, including ballast voyages,

excluding periods during which reduction of speed for safety, congestion,

narrow/restricted water and/or river-canal passage and/or reduced visibility

and/or sea passages from safe berth to safe berth up to 24 hrs after/ prior

cosp/eosp and/or periods when speed is reduced due to charterers or pilots

orders.

Weather routing service, if employed, to be notified of the applicability of

the word ''about '' to both the consumptions and speed, applying a 5 %

upwards in the consumption and 5% downwards in the speed.

Present fixture to be performed only with the speed and consumption as

described in vessel's description and Owners have the right to reject

Charterer's request for any other different speed, even if same is in WOG

condition regarding vessel's consumption.

Owners are entitled to a credit for any bunker savings, which are to be

calculated as the difference between maximum quantities of bunkers the ship

was allowed to consume as per above and the quantity of bunkers calculated

by taking the average bunkers consumed during good weather days during the

voyage extrapolated over the complete voyage.

Owners are entitled to the said credit for bunker savings irrespective of

whether there is any speed claim. In the event that there is a speed claim,

the credit for bunker savings shall be set-off against any loss of time to

reduce or extinguish speed claim, and in the event bunkers savings are of

value greater than speed claim, owners are entitled to credit for remaining

balance of bunker savings after setting off speed claim.

Owners are entitled to a credit for any time saved, which is to be

calculated as the difference between maximum time the ship was allowed to

complete the voyage as per above and the time the time calculated by taking

the average speed during good weather days during the voyage extrapolated

over the complete voyage.

Owners are entitled to the said credit for time saved irrespective of

whether there is any over-consumption claim. In the event that there is an

over-consumption claim, the credit for time saved shall be set-off against

any over-consumption to reduce or extinguish over-consumption claim, and in

the event time saved is of value greater than over-consumption claim, owners

are entitled to credit for remaining balance of time saved after setting off

over-consumption claim.

In the event that there are no good weather days, owners shall be entitled

to calculate bunker savings and/or time saved for purposes of said credits

as the difference between actual speed and/or consumption in all weathers

during the voyage and maximum time the ship was allowed to complete the

voyage and/or maximum quantities of bunkers the ship was allowed to consume

during the voyage.

FUEL OIL SPECIFICATION / TREATMENT:

The Charterers shall supply only suitable fuels as per specification to

enable main propulsion and auxiliary machinery as well as boiler to operate

efficiently and without harmful effects.

Fuels to be mineral oil based products of stable and homogeneous nature and

shall comply, be delivered and sampled in accordance with international,

regional or national regulations and latest CIMAC recommendations and ISO

standard 8217:2017, ISO/PAS 23263:2019 and MARPOL Annex VI (or any

subsequent amendments of the above); and shall not contain waste lubricants,

tar oil, inorganic acids, chemicals or any other harmful substances.

When the vessel is ordered to trade within emission control zones (ECA), it

is understood that the vessel's engines to burn DMZ ultra-low sulphur MGO or

ULSHFO of max 0,1% sulphur content, else as per ISO 8217:2017; charterers

to supply bunkers in compliance to fuel sulphur contents requirements. If

charterers intend to supply biofuels, they must comply with ISO 8217:2024

specifications.

Comingling of ULSMGO supplies only is allowed.

Change-over procedures from HS fuel to ULS fuel will depend to ULS fuel's

sulphur content and in any case not to be less than 2 hours prior entering

an ECA.

Charterers have no right whatsoever to claim for loss of speed and bunkers

over-consumption during the change-over period.

Bimco Fueleu maritime clause for TC parties 2024 to apply.

The Charterers shall be responsible to purchase and transfer into the

Owners' nominated Emission Scheme account the Emission Allowances (EUA)

corresponding to the Vessel's emissions under the scope of the applicable

EU-ETS (European Union Emissions Trading System) following the EU Directive

EU 2023/959 - EU 2003/87 EC throughout the Charter Party period even when

vessel has been delivered at sea (i.e passing) or at non eu port but with

last port of cargo operation being eu port.

cii operations clause for TC parties 2022 to apply.

Bunker sampling as per ISO 13739:2020

BALLASTING:

For the safe performance of the vessel, when in long ballast voyage to cross

oceans, it is master's absolute discretion to heavily ballast the ship,

including flooding of No 3 hold. Ballasting/de-ballasting time, if any, as

well as any drying-up time, to be for Charterers' account. Ballasting/

de-ballasting of hold no 3 to be effected / executed in a safe port or

safe/sheltered anchorage, not en route during sailing. It is not allowed to

partially ballast cargo hold no 3 for any reason. Ballasting/de-ballasting

of hold No 3 is not to take place for reasons other than the above.

Vessel has the liberty of steaming at slow speed or stop/being idle for the

purposes of ballast exchange, if required. In case that the water Salinity

during ballast operations is less than 0.9PSU(1050 S/cm)Limit for BWTS

Operation, ,vessel has the liberty to Ballast with untreated ballast and to

Replace same at open sea basis IMO and local regulations or to carry her

APT full with open sea salt water in order to use it for increase salinity

and treat her ballast while at port with low salinity . Cost of discharging

untreated ballast if required and any loss of speed /time to be for

charterers account

ANY PERIODS WHERE VESSEL SPEED IS REDUCED DUE TO NARROW/RESTRICTED WATERS, POOR VISIBILITY, FOR THE SAFETY OF THE VESSEL, OR DUE TO CHARTERERS INSTRUCTIONS OR WHEN THE VESSEL HAS TO FOLLOW RECOMMENDED AND/OR MANDATED SPEEDS WHICH MAY BE SUGGESTED BY LOCAL AUTHORITIES, ARE TO BE EXCLUDED FROM THE ANALYSIS AND ARE NOT SUBJECT TO ANY PERFORMANCE CLAIM. SIMILARLY, ANY OVER-CONSUMPTION CAN BE OFFSET BY ANY TIME GAINED BUT IN ANY CASE, OVERALL PERFORMANCE TO BE CALCULATED FOR BOTH LADEN AND BALLAST LEGS. THE VESSEL IS FITTED WITH AN OPEN LOOP SCRUBBER SYSTEM, WHICH CHARTERERS ARE ALLOWED TO USE STRICTLY AS PER CHARTER PARTY TERMS AND PROVISIONS.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM CAN BE ONLY USED WHERE IS ALLOWED BY LOCAL/NATIONAL AUTHORITIES AND REGULATIONS AND ALWAYS IN ACCORDANCE WITH MARPOL REGULATIONS (OTHERWISE COMPLIANT FUEL ON BOARD SHOULD BE USED), AND WHERE CAN ONLY BE SAFELY OPERATED AND SUBJECT TO SEAWATER SALINITY.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM NOT TO BE USED IN RIVERS, CANALS, INLAND AND/OR MUDDY AND/OR RESTRICTED AND/OR LOW ALKALINITY LEVEL WATERS.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM ALSO NOT TO BE USED WHILE ENTERING A PORT, WHILE LEAVING A PORT, DURING PORT STAY, WHENEVER MANOEUVRING, DURING RIVER NAVIGATION, WHILE TRANSITING SUEZ AND PANAMA CANAL AND WHEN NAVIGATING/TRADING IN THE LOCATIONS BELOW:

PORT HEDLAND,

GREAT BARRIER REEF,

TORRES STRAIT,

SINGAPORE/MALACCA STRAIT,

ENGLISH CHANNEL/DOVER STRAIT,

BALTIC STRAITS, AS WELL AS IN ANY OTHER CONFINED AREAS WHERE THE MASTER CONSIDERS UNSAFE (IN ANY RESPECT) TO USE THE SCRUBBER.

IT IS ALSO EXPRESSLY UNDERSTOOD THAT CHARTERERS ARE NOT PERMITTED TO ORDER THE VESSEL TO ANY PORT OR JURISDICTION WHERE THE EXISTENCE ON BOARD AND/OR THE USE OF THE EGCS/SCRUBBER IS PROHIBITED, IRRESPECTIVE OF OPERATION, OR IF OPERATING THE SCRUBBER WOULD VIOLATE APPLICABLE LAWS OR REGULATIONS IN THE VESSEL'S RELEVANT VOYAGE AND TRADING LIMITS, AND THE MASTER CAN LEGITIMATELY REFUSE SUCH AN EMPLOYMENT ORDER.

SUBJECT TO OWNERS PRIOR APPROVAL, IF THE CHARTERERS REQUIRE THE VESSEL TO OPERATE THE SCRUBBER TO SCRUB DOWN TO 0.1% SULPHUR CONTENT, THE CHARTERERS WILL THEN COMPENSATE OWNERS WITH THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HSFO AND LSMGO PRICES AS PER THE CHARTER PARTY FOR THE FUEL SO SCRUBBED DOWN.

Speed and Consumption:

About 12 kts on about 21.50 mt HSFO + 0.2 mt LSMGO laden up to summer draft / about 12.5 kts on about 19,50 mt HSFO + 0.2 mt LSMGO in light ballast, basis up to max Beaufort Force 4 and/or Douglas Sea State 3 of maximum swell/waves height 1.25metres and basis no adverse currents, with favourable currents (if any) to be excluded from a voyage evaluation.

Vessel is allowed every 2/3 days to increase rpm at Master's discretion for about 2/3 hours with additional consumption for Charterers account.

The speed and consumption so described is on average basis taken over the entire charter period, including ballast voyage(s), provided no deck cargo on board and provided no underwater parts fouling due to vessel remaining at or shifting (for any purpose) within a place, anchorage(s) and/or berth(s) or combined for an aggregated period of 15 or more days. No shifting and/or sailing within or outside the geographical limits of a port or from one port to another for less than continuous 48 hours should be treated as breaking the continuous running of 15 days. In such a case, it is a condition precedent to redelivery that Charterers to remain responsible for inspecting/cleaning (at Charterers' time, risk and expense) the vessel's under water parts and super polishing the propeller prior to redelivery. Cleaning to be to the Owners' satisfaction while the cleaning company as well as the equipment to be used to be approved by Owners. Performance is on the basis of 24 consecutive hours available good weather.

The speed and consumption parameters provided in this description are not subject to review by charterers who are expressly prohibited from instructing Master to proceed at a speed and/or with a consumption other than as provided herein. All methods of assessing the vessel's performance during the charter, other than the good weather method, are excluded.

Vessel burns LSMGO whilst manoeuvring, also whilst in restricted or narrow or confined waters or in waters with restricted visibility.

PORT CONSUMPTION WHERE SCRUBBERS CAN BE USED:

Port consumption idle about 3 MT HSFO + 1 MT LSMGO per day.

Port consumption in cold or very warm weather add 1 MT HSFO per day extra.

The above consumptions at Sea and in Port exclude HSFO or LSMGO consumed for ballasting/deballasting and exchange of ballast, hold cleaning and washing, closing/opening of hatches and bilge pumping.

Charterers to maintain sufficient quantity of HSFO and LSMGO on board for such purposes.

CONSUMPTION AT SEA AND IN PORT IN PORTS/PLACES/AREAS WHERE SCRUBBER CANNOT BE USED: ALL FIGURES AS PER ABOVE EXCEPT FOR VESSEL TO BURN ONLY LMSGO (max 0.1% sc), INSTEAD OF HSFO.

All fuels supplied shall be homogenous and of quality suitable for burning in the vessel's engines and auxiliaries and to conform to ISO 8217:2010(E) specifications (and any amendments thereto) of quality RMG380 for IFO 380cst (HSFO ) with max sulphur content of 3.5% and which to be compliant with 2020 "sulphur content requirements" i.e. any sulphur content and related requirements as stipulated in marpol annex vi (as amended from time to time) and/or by any other applicable lawful authority and DMA for LSMGO. No mixing of fuels is allowed. ULO (Used Lubrication Oil) or other waste products are strictly not permitted and not to be included in the fuels supplied. LSMGO supplied to have max SULPHUR CONTENT OF 0.1%.

Charterers warrant that they will not bunker the vessel with bunkers by Iranian or Russian suppliers or with fuels/bunkers of Iranian or Russian origin. Bunkering by Iranian or Russian suppliers or bunkering in Iran or Russia or bunkering with fuels of Iranian or Russian origin is strictly prohibited.

- SPEED / CONS ARE ABT IN GOOD WEATHER UP TO MAX BF 4 AND SMOOTH SEA CONDITIONS AND NO ADVERSE CURRENTS OR NEGATIVE INFLUENCE OF SWELL, ON SSW DRAFT, NOT IN CONGESTED AREAS OR PASSING THROUGH STRAITS OR AREAS WITH POOR VISIBILITY AND PROVIDED NO BOTTOM FOULING DUE TO IDLE PERIODS OF 22 DAYS OR MORE. AN ADMISSIBLE PERIOD OF GOOD WEATHER HAS TO BE A PERIOD OF 24 CONSECUTIVE HOURS RUNNING FROM NOON TO NOON. EXTRAPOLATION OF "GOOD WEATHER" PERFORMANCE FOR "BAD WEATHER" PERIODS IS NOT ALLOWED. ADVERSE CURRENT HAS TO BE TAKEN INTO ACCOUNT WHEN ASSESSING VESSEL'S SPEED AND CONSUMPTION. HOWEVER, FAVORABLE CURRENTS ARE TO BE EXPRESSLY EXCLUDED FROM VESSEL'S PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS. TOTAL TIME LOST AND OVER CONSUMED BUNKERS CALCULATIONS SHALL BE BASED ON THE GOOD WEATHER PERIODS ONLY, MEANING THAT THE AVERAGE GOOD WEATHER PERFORMANCE CANNOT THEN BE EXTENDED AND APPLIED OVER THE WHOLE CHARTER DURATION INCLUDING BAD WEATHER. BAD WEATHER PERIODS TO BE DISREGARDED WHEN CALCULATING THE TIME LOST AND THE BUNKERS OVER CONSUMED THROUGH THE CHARTER PERIOD THAN THE WARRANTED TIME & CONSUMPTION, DURING GOOD/BAD WEATHER PERIOD IF VSL UNDER CONSUME THAN THE WARRANTED MAXIMUM CONSUMPTION AND OVERPERFORMED THAN THE MINIMUM WARRANTED SPEED IN THAT CASE OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO CREDIT FOR TIME & BUNKER SAVED. IN CASES WHEN VOYAGES ARE FROM SEA BUOY TO SEA BUOY ON VOYAGES LESS THAN 48 HOURS AND 24 HOURS AFTER COSP/PRIOR EOSP OR COASTAL TRADE, ACTUAL CONSUMPTION FIGURES TO APPLY.

VESSEL HAS THE LIBERTY TO USE LSMGO FOR THE MAIN ENGINE WHEN SECA, ECA, MANOEUVERING IN/OUT OF PORTS, CANALS, RIVERS, RESTRICTED AREAS, SHALLOW & NARROW WATERS, FOR STARTING UP/TURNING ENGINES, BURNING SLUDGE AND IN ADVERSE WEATHER, BAD/COLD WEATHER, HOLD CLEANING AND BILGE PUMPING. MASTER HAS THE AUTHORITY TO DIVERT COURSE FOR SMOOTH RUNNING/SAFETY OF THE VESSEL. SPEED AND CONSUMPTION FIGURES IN FAIR WEATHER WITHOUT ADVERSE CURRENT, POOR VISIBILITY, CONGESTION, NO NEGATIVE INFLUENCE OF SWELL UP TO BEAUFORT SCALE FORCE 4 / DOUGLAS SEA STATE 3 / WAVE AND SWELL COMBINED HEIGHTS UPTO 1.25 M. ABOUT ON SPEED AND CONSUMPTION CLARIFIED AS MEANING 5% ALLOWANCE ON CONSUMPTION AND 0.5 KNOTS ON SPEED, BOTH IN OWNERS FAVOR. IT IS UNDERSTOOD THAT VESSEL NOT TO BE ORDERED TO SAIL ON ECONOMICAL SPEED/CONSUMPTION IN PIRACY RISKS AREAS

ALL DETAILS ABOUT AND GIVEN IN GOOD FAITH ANY SAVING IN CONSUMPTION MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY SPEED UNDERPERFORMANCE, ANY SAVING IN TIME MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY EXCESS CONSUMPTION, ANY SAVING IN FO MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST INCREASED MDO AND VICE VERSA, AND ANY OVERALL SAVING ON INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S) MUST BE SET OFF AGAINST AN OVERALL LOSS ON OTHER INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S). OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO A CREDIT FOR ANY BUNKER SAVINGS, WHICH ARE TO BE CALCULATED AS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN MAXIMUM QUANTITIES OF BUNKERS ALLOWED TO CONSUME AS PER ABOVE AND THE QUANTITY OF BUNKERS ACTUALLY CONSUMED DURING THE VOYAGE. THE PRICE OF THE BUNKER TO BE AS PER CP PRICE AGREED DURING NEGO. OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO THE SAID CREDIT FOR BUNKER SAVINGS IRRESPECTIVE OF WHETHER THERE IS ANY SPEED CLAIM. IN THE EVENT THAT THERE IS A SPEED CLAIM, THE CREDIT FOR BUNKER SAVINGS SHALL BE SET-OFF AGAINST ANY LOSS OF TIME TO REDUCE OR EXTINGUISH SPEED CLAIM, AND IN THE EVENT BUNKERS SAVINGS ARE OF VALUE GREATER THAN SPEED CLAIM, OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO CREDIT FOR REMAINING BALANCE OF BUNKER SAVINGS AFTER SETTING OFF SPEED CLAIM.OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO A CREDIT FOR ANY TIME SAVED, WHICH IS TO BE CALCULATED AS THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN MAXIMUM TIME THE SHIP WAS ALLOWED TO COMPLETE THE VOYAGE AS PER ABOVE AND THE TIME ACTUALLY TAKEN TO COMPLETE THE VOYAGE.OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO THE SAID CREDIT FOR TIME SAVED IRRESPECTIVE OF WHETHER THERE IS ANY OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM. IN THE EVENT THAT THERE IS AN OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, THE CREDIT FOR TIME SAVED SHALL BE SET-OFF AGAINST ANY OVER-CONSUMPTION TO REDUCE OR EXTINGUISH OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, AND IN THE EVENT TIME SAVED IS OF VALUE GREATER THAN OVER-CONSUMPTION CLAIM, OWNERS ARE ENTITLED TO CREDIT FOR REMAINING BALANCE OF TIME SAVED AFTER SETTING OFF OVER- CONSUMPTION CLAIM.

THE VESSEL IS FITTED WITH AN OPEN LOOP SCRUBBER SYSTEM, WHICH CHARTERERS ARE

ALLOWED TO USE STRICTLY AS PER CHARTER PARTY TERMS AND PROVISIONS.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM CAN BE ONLY USED WHERE IS ALLOWED BY LOCAL/NATIONAL

AUTHORITIES AND REGULATIONS AND ALWAYS IN ACCORDANCE WITH MARPOL REGULATIONS

(OTHERWISE COMPLIANT FUEL ON BOARD SHOULD BE USED), AND WHERE CAN ONLY BE

SAFELY OPERATED AND SUBJECT TO SEAWATER SALINITY.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM NOT TO BE USED IN RIVERS, CANALS, INLAND AND/OR MUDDY

AND/OR RESTRICTED AND/OR LOW ALKALINITY LEVEL WATERS.

THE SCRUBBER SYSTEM ALSO NOT TO BE USED WHILE ENTERING A PORT, WHILE LEAVING

A PORT, DURING PORT STAY, WHENEVER MANOEUVRING, DURING RIVER NAVIGATION,

WHILE TRANSITING SUEZ AND PANAMA CANAL AND WHEN NAVIGATING/TRADING IN THE

LOCATIONS BELOW:

PORT HEDLAND,

GREAT BARRIER REEF,

TORRES STRAIT,

SINGAPORE/MALACCA STRAIT,

ENGLISH CHANNEL/DOVER STRAIT,

BALTIC STRAITS, AS WELL AS IN ANY OTHER CONFINED AREAS WHERE THE MASTER

CONSIDERS UNSAFE (IN ANY RESPECT) TO USE THE SCRUBBER.

IT IS ALSO EXPRESSLY UNDERSTOOD THAT CHARTERERS ARE NOT PERMITTED TO ORDER

THE VESSEL TO ANY PORT OR JURISDICTION WHERE THE EXISTENCE ON BOARD AND/OR

THE USE OF THE EGCS/SCRUBBER IS PROHIBITED, IRRESPECTIVE OF OPERATION, OR IF

OPERATING THE SCRUBBER WOULD VIOLATE APPLICABLE LAWS OR REGULATIONS IN THE

VESSEL'S RELEVANT VOYAGE AND TRADING LIMITS, AND THE MASTER CAN LEGITIMATELY

REFUSE SUCH AN EMPLOYMENT ORDER.

SUBJECT TO OWNERS PRIOR APPROVAL, IF THE CHARTERERS REQUIRE THE VESSEL TO

OPERATE THE SCRUBBER TO SCRUB DOWN TO 0.1% SULPHUR CONTENT, THE CHARTERERS

WILL THEN COMPENSATE OWNERS WITH THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN HSFO AND LSMGO

PRICES AS PER THE CHARTER PARTY FOR THE FUEL SO SCRUBBED DOWN.

Speed and Consumption:

About 12 kts on about 21.50 mt HSFO + 0.2 mt LSMGO laden up to summer draft

/ about 12.5 kts on about 19,50 mt HSFO + 0.2 mt LSMGO in light ballast,

basis up to max Beaufort Force 4 and/or Douglas Sea State 3 of maximum

swell/waves height 1.25metres and basis no adverse currents, with favourable

currents (if any) to be excluded from a voyage evaluation.

Vessel is allowed every 2/3 days to increase rpm at Master's discretion for

about 2/3 hours with additional consumption for Charterers account.

The speed and consumption so described is on average basis taken over the

entire charter period, including ballast voyage(s), provided no deck cargo

on board and provided no underwater parts fouling due to vessel remaining at

or shifting (for any purpose) within a place, anchorage(s) and/or berth(s)

or combined for an aggregated period of 15 or more days. No shifting and/or

sailing within or outside the geographical limits of a port or from one port

to another for less than continuous 48 hours should be treated as breaking

the continuous running of 15 days. In such a case, it is a condition

precedent to redelivery that Charterers to remain responsible for

inspecting/cleaning (at Charterers' time, risk and expense) the vessel's

under water parts and super polishing the propeller prior to redelivery.

Cleaning to be to the Owners' satisfaction while the cleaning company as

well as the equipment to be used to be approved by Owners. Performance is on

the basis of 24 consecutive hours available good weather.

The speed and consumption parameters provided in this description are not

subject to review by charterers who are expressly prohibited from

instructing Master to proceed at a speed and/or with a consumption other

than as provided herein. All methods of assessing the vessel's performance

during the charter, other than the good weather method, are excluded.

Vessel burns LSMGO whilst manoeuvring, also whilst in restricted or narrow

or confined waters or in waters with restricted visibility.

PORT CONSUMPTION WHERE SCRUBBERS CAN BE USED:

Port consumption idle about 3 MT HSFO + 1 MT LSMGO per day.

Port consumption in cold or very warm weather add 1 MT HSFO per day extra.

The above consumptions at Sea and in Port exclude HSFO or LSMGO consumed for

ballasting/deballasting and exchange of ballast, hold cleaning and washing,

closing/opening of hatches and bilge pumping.

Charterers to maintain sufficient quantity of HSFO and LSMGO on board for

such purposes.

CONSUMPTION AT SEA AND IN PORT IN PORTS/PLACES/AREAS WHERE SCRUBBER CANNOT

BE USED: ALL FIGURES AS PER ABOVE EXCEPT FOR VESSEL TO BURN ONLY LMSGO (max

0.1% sc), INSTEAD OF HSFO.

All fuels supplied shall be homogenous and of quality suitable for burning

in the vessel's engines and auxiliaries and to conform to ISO 8217:2010(E)

specifications (and any amendments thereto) of quality RMG380 for IFO 380cst

(HSFO ) with max sulphur content of 3.5% and which to be compliant with 2020

"sulphur content requirements" i.e. any sulphur content and related

requirements as stipulated in marpol annex vi (as amended from time to time)

and/or by any other applicable lawful authority and DMA for LSMGO. No mixing

of fuels is allowed. ULO (Used Lubrication Oil) or other waste products are

strictly not permitted and not to be included in the fuels supplied. LSMGO

supplied to have max SULPHUR CONTENT OF 0.1%.

Charterers warrant that they will not bunker the vessel with bunkers by

Iranian or Russian suppliers or with fuels/bunkers of Iranian or Russian

origin. Bunkering by Iranian or Russian suppliers or bunkering in Iran or

Russia or bunkering with fuels of Iranian or Russian origin is strictly

prohibited.

When seawater temperature exceeds 32.0 degC for more than 6 hours in any

noon report period a speed

loss of 0.5 knots is expected if sailing and an additional fuel consumption

of 0.5 mt is expected if in port due

to extreme seawater cooling system demands.

The vessel shall have the liberty to use GO for maneuvering on

departure/arrival, starting of A/E, navigating

in shallow/restricted/busy waters, canals, and rivers. Full speed will be

reduced during running in program

of main engine cylinder liners as per engine manufacturers recommendations.

Warranted speed and consumption applies in good weather conditions up to and

including Beaufort wind

force 4 and Douglas sea state 3 (maximum significant wave height of 1.25 m)

free from adverse effects of

currents and swell, even keel at summer draft or less, and excluding voyages

less than 48 hours transit time

from sea pilot to sea pilot, periods during reductions of speed for safety,

sailing in piracy areas, passages

with restricted visibility, congested waters, straits, narrowed waters, and

while approaching, leaving,

entering, and maneuvering within ports, and in extreme hot/cold climates.

Positive current shall not be

used in diminishing vessel's performance. About qualifies both speed and

consumption, and means up to

5% additional fuel consumption and up to 0.5 knots lower speed. Speed

underperformance to be offset

against under-consumption.

Periods of less than 24 hours, from noon report to noon report, of

continuous good weather to be expressly

excluded from determination of the vessel's performance. Any performance

claims are to be based on good

weather performance only, and when at least twenty percent of the sea days

are good weather days.

and LSMGO (max 0.10%

sulfur). During the currency of this charter, Charterers are only permitted

to supply these grades to the

Greetings,

For your Reference: Daily News Report (27/11/2025)

Oil Price: Brent rises to $63 as Russia tempers expectations of a ceasefire

Brent inches higher to $63 amid doubts on potential Ukraine war peace deal

Brent rose to $63 on Thursday as doubts grew over a potential Ukraine-Russia

ceasefire coming into fruition after a Russian diplomat said on Wednesday

Moscow would offer no major concessions, dimming prospects of greater

Russian supply returning to the market

China issues first batch of 2026 crude import quotas for independent

refiners

A Reuters report on Thursday revealed China has issued nearly 8 million

tonnes of 2026 crude import quotas to independent refiners, up from 6.04

million tonnes in November 2024, for cargoes arriving by the end of 2025

Pemex crude production declines in October

Official data released on Wednesday showed Mexican state energy firm Pemex's

crude oil production declined 4.4% y-o-y to 1.64 million b/d in October,

attributing the decline to the lack of relevant discoveries and natural

decline of key fields

From: EastGate Research <research@eastgate.gr>

Sent: Friday, 28 November 2025 3:52 PM

To: _ EastGate Research <research@eastgate.gr>

Subject: Commencement of operations at the Simandou mine unlocks a new era of long-haul iron ore flows

TELiX MSG: 05ZPY-00 28/11/25 13:54 +02:00

<https://www.eastgate.gr/>

28th November 2025

SHIPPING NEWS UPDATE

Commencement of operations at the Simandou mine unlocks a new era of long-haul iron ore flows

The emergence of the Guinea-China long-haul iron ore trade will structurally support global CAPESIZE demand, supporting freight rates across all major routes and creating one of the strongest tonne-mile expansions in recent dry bulk history. It will also help China to reduce its reliance on Australia to some extent.

Simandou is one of the world's biggest and highest-grade iron (65% Fe content) ore deposits, located in southeast Guinea. The first iron ore shipment from the Simandou project was shipped in November 2025. The ore was transported from the SimFer mine via the newly constructed Trans-Guinea railway to the Morebaya port, one of the key export facilities built as part of the Simandou project, and loaded onto a bulk carrier for shipment to China.

Simandou is divided into two zones: Blocks 1-2 are operated by the China-aligned WCS consortium and have a reserve of 1.8 billion tonnes, while Blocks 3-4 are led by the Rio Tinto-Chalco joint venture, having reserves of 1.5 billion tonnes, with Guinea holding a 15% stake. Although Rio Tinto is a major partner in the southern blocks, Chinese entities collectively have the largest strategic influence across the entire project through both mine ownership and shared control of the rail-port infrastructure. China is the largest combined strategic stakeholder across all four blocks.

China's shifting iron ore import strategy

In Brazil, Vale supplies more than 80% of the country's iron ore and has shipping arrangements with COSCO and other Chinese companies.

Therefore, a spike in iron ore imports from Guinea is unlikely to cause a decline in the iron ore trade on the Brazil-China route. Since China is involved in Guinea's iron ore investment, most of the Guinean iron ore is likely to be exported to China. Meanwhile, China's political relations with Australia have been strained, and it is more probable that a rise in Guinea's iron ore supply will prompt Chinese importers to shift away from Australia to Guinea, boosting shipping demand.

Additionally, high-Fe content iron ore provide the high-grade input required for low-emission steel, making it crucial for the global green steel transition.

DRI-EAF and hydrogen-based steelmaking needs DR-grade ore (67%+ Fe), but such ore is extremely scarce, and only a small share of the global seaborne market meets this threshold. Without high-grade ore, green hydrogen DRI cannot scale. Net-zero pathways (2050) call for a major shift from blast furnaces to DRI-EAF. The structural shortage of DR-grade projects makes high-grade deposits such as Simandou (65% Fe, near DR-grade) strategically critical for green steel.

With trade routes getting longer, Capesizes get stronger

Assumption: A spike in imports from Guinea will lead to an equivalent decline in China's iron ore imports from Australia.

The shift in iron ore trade flows from Australia-China to Guinea-China will boost the vessel utilisation of Capesizes. Once Simandou reaches its targeted 120 million tonnes of annual exports, most likely by 2030, the Guinea-China route will require almost 180 Capesizes compared to 64 vessels needed to transport the same volume from Australia due to the significantly shorter sailing distance. The Guinea-China round voyage is more than 90 days, almost three times the 30-35 days Australia-China rotation.

As a result, each CAPESIZE operating on this route completes fewer voyages per year, increasing vessel demand for the same volume of trade. This shift in trade will lead to a net demand of 116 additional CAPESIZE vessels, representing one of the largest single tonne-mile expansions anticipated in the dry bulk sector in this decade.

Although the freight rate between Australia-China is one-third compared to Guinea-China, making Guinea's ore seemingly more expensive, China is still likely to prioritise sourcing from Guinea. This is because Guinea will supply high-grade, low-impurity iron ore (65% Fe), a similar edge that Brazil has, compared to Australia's predominantly 59-61% Fe ores, which requires more blending to meet the required quality standards.

Consequently, the shipping cost structure of Brazil and Guinea will be broadly comparable, and once Simandou reaches full-scale production, its cost curve is expected to be similar to Brazil (if not low). Moreover, China's objective of diversifying away from Australian supply, combined with its massive investment in Guinea's rail and port infrastructure, makes economic and strategic sense.

This will push charter rates up on major routes and not only on West Africa-China, as fewer ships remain available for flexible deployment. Overall, the development of Simandou will create a structurally stronger CAPESIZE freight market, with strong earnings across global routes for years to come.

Source: Drewry

Commencement of operations at the Simandou mine unlocks a new era of long-haul iron ore flows

The emergence of the Guinea-China long-haul iron ore trade will structurally support global CAPESIZE demand, supporting freight rates across all major routes and creating one of the strongest tonne-mile expansions in recent dry bulk history. It will also help China to reduce its reliance on Australia to some extent.

Simandou is one of the world's biggest and highest-grade iron (65% Fe content) ore deposits, located in southeast Guinea. Although Rio Tinto is a major partner in the southern blocks, Chinese entities collectively have the largest strategic influence across the entire project through both mine ownership and shared control of the rail-port infrastructure. China is the largest combined strategic stakeholder across all four blocks.

China's shifting iron ore import strategy

In Brazil, Vale supplies more than 80% of the country's iron ore and has shipping arrangements with COSCO and other Chinese companies.

Therefore, a spike in iron ore imports from Guinea is unlikely to cause a decline in the iron ore trade on the Brazil-China route. Since China is involved in Guinea's iron ore investment, most of the Guinean iron ore is likely to be exported to China. Meanwhile, China's political relations with Australia have been strained, and it is more probable that a rise in Guinea's iron ore supply will prompt Chinese importers to shift away from Australia to Guinea, boosting shipping demand.

Additionally, high-Fe content iron ore provide the high-grade input required for low-emission steel, making it crucial for the global green steel transition.

DRI-EAF and hydrogen-based steelmaking needs DR-grade ore (67%+ Fe), but such ore is extremely scarce, and only a small share of the global seaborne market meets this threshold. Without high-grade ore, green hydrogen DRI cannot scale. Net-zero pathways (2050) call for a major shift from blast furnaces to DRI-EAF. The structural shortage of DR-grade projects makes high-grade deposits such as Simandou (65% Fe, near DR-grade) strategically critical for green steel.

With trade routes getting longer, Capesizes get stronger

Assumption: A spike in imports from Guinea will lead to an equivalent decline in China's iron ore imports from Australia.

The shift in iron ore trade flows from Australia-China to Guinea-China will boost the vessel utilisation of Capesizes. Once Simandou reaches its targeted 120 million tonnes of annual exports, most likely by 2030, the Guinea-China route will require almost 180 Capesizes compared to 64 vessels needed to transport the same volume from Australia due to the significantly shorter sailing distance. The Guinea-China round voyage is more than 90 days, almost three times the 30-35 days Australia-China rotation.

As a result, each CAPESIZE operating on this route completes fewer voyages per year, increasing vessel demand for the same volume of trade. This shift in trade will lead to a net demand of 116 additional CAPESIZE vessels, representing one of the largest single tonne-mile expansions anticipated in the dry bulk sector in this decade.

Although the freight rate between Australia-China is one-third compared to Guinea-China, making Guinea's ore seemingly more expensive, China is still likely to prioritise sourcing from Guinea. This is because Guinea will supply high-grade, low-impurity iron ore (65% Fe), a similar edge that Brazil has, compared to Australia's predominantly 59-61% Fe ores, which requires more blending to meet the required quality standards.

Consequently, the shipping cost structure of Brazil and Guinea will be broadly comparable, and once Simandou reaches full-scale production, its cost curve is expected to be similar to Brazil (if not low). Moreover, China's objective of diversifying away from Australian supply, combined with its massive investment in Guinea's rail and port infrastructure, makes economic and strategic sense.

This will push charter rates up on major routes and not only on West Africa-China, as fewer ships remain available for flexible deployment. Overall, the development of Simandou will create a structurally stronger CAPESIZE freight market, with strong earnings across global routes for years to come.

SLOW STEAMING CONDITIONS FROM NCR TO MIN 50% OF MCR:

1) M/E LOAD (%) SHOULD BE ALWAYS MAINTAINED OVER 50 % ALWAYS (I.E. NOT LESS

THAN THE RANGE OF 49 %) OF MCR OUTPUT IN SAFE OPERABLE CONDITION AND

WRITEUP

CAPESIZE

The week opened on a positive footing, with the BCI 5TC adding $682 to

settle at $37,840. The Pacific set a constructive tone early on, supported

by two miners in the market and a steady flow of operator demand. Fixtures

on C5 were reported in the $12.00-$12.05 range, nudging the C5 index up by

0.185 to $12.075. The Atlantic, by contrast, saw a more muted start to the

week, with most participants opting to watch and assess following last

week's activity. Still, the underlying sentiment remained firm, helped in

part by reports of $25.50 paid on C3 last Friday for December cancelling,

which pushed the C3 index up by 0.191 to $25.282.

Atlantic

Classic fixed on Friday a Oldendorff TBN for 190,000/10 from Tubarao option

West Africa to China for December cancelling at $25.50, lacking further

details.

Asia

BHP fixed a TBN for 160,000/10 from Port Hedland to Qingdao for 17/19

December at $12.00, the Captial controlled Amorito (179,000 2012) was linked

to this. Rio Tinto fixed a TBN for 170,000/10 from Dampier to Qingdao for

16/18 December at $12.05, the Jera controlled Cape Peregrine (180,000 2012)

was linked to this.

PANAMAX

The week opened on a softer footing, with sentiment easing as owners' in

both the Atlantic and Asia came forward with prompt tonnage looking to

secure cover. This has led to increased negotiability on the offer side,

shifting the tone from last week's firmness. In the Atlantic, positions have

begun to build, tempering previous support from the U.S. Gulf and East Coast

with some cargoes showing internal support from internal tonnage coverage. A

similar pattern is emerging in Asia, where more prompt vessels are now

competing for a steadier flow of cargo. Although still early in the week,

the Pacific no longer appears set for the same momentum as before, even on

Indonesian trades. Period activity was limited and the P5TC average closed

$161 down at $17,405.

Atlantic

Messrs Damico were reported to have fixed the Silvergate (77,239 2014) at

$29,000 for trip basis aps E.C. South America 15 December for a trip

redelivery Skaw-Gibraltar. The MP KAMSARMAX 1 (81,190 2017) which sailed PMO

on the 19 November was reported to have fixcd E.C. South America into SE

asia but no other details emerged.

Asia

The Primrose Atlantic (81,554 2016) open Kakogawa 30 November fixed a trip

from Port Latta into China with intention iron ore at $20,500 to Richland.

Although full confirmation and subsequent details remain, it is said that

the Cl Taizhou (81,119 2019) Taichung 2/3 December fixed a trip via

Indonesia into South Korea with KLC. Similarly, the Crimson Amber (81,703

2019) Taichung 3 December was reported to have fixed, but no other details

surfaced.

SUPRAMAX

A typical Monday with very little in the way of fresh information. From the

Atlantic, the US Gulf continued to lose ground although some felt a bottom

may have been reached, however no fresh fixture details came to light. The

South Atlantic remained finely balanced again little sign of any change in

fundamentals. From the Mediterranean some felt that there was a bit more

interest from the East MED, but again fixing remained scarce. The Asian

arena was a somewhat more positive affair and most routes started in

positive territory, although it is still early in the week and a clear

direction was a little hard to call. The 11TC average closed up slightly

from Friday by $13 to settle at $18,228.

HANDYSIZE

It was a typical Monday with slow start to the week. The BHSI edged up by 3

points to 830, while the 7TC average increased by $47 to close at $14,932.

In the Atlantic, the Continent and Mediterranean regions held steady,

supported by tight tonnage. The South Atlantic also remained firm, with

limited vessel supply and consistent demand pushing rates above previous

levels. Activity in the U.S. Gulf was minimal as the market settles back in

after the Thanksgiving holiday, with most fresh inquiries appearing to be

charterers in "collecting" mode for the time being. Across Asia, the market

was similarly muted, with direction dependent on the pace of fresh demand.

Some period interest was noted, largely driven by positive sentiment from

the spot market. In terms of fixtures, the Clipper Bari Star (37,976 2023)

was fixed on delivery Continent for a 5-6 month period redelivery

Singapore-Japan at $15,500 by Oldendorff, but further details were not

disclosed.

Subject: NSI Daily Market Report 01/12/2025

Hi Deepesh

We have opened form this morning but still trading within a tight range.

The losses on Friday were mostly attributed to the market's impatience over

the ceasefire negotiations between Russia and Ukraine, perceiving that the

talks to formalise a peace deal is being drawn-out. The founder and chief

executive officer of XAnalysts Pty, Mukesh Sahdev, commented that "It may

take some time for a potential Ukraine-Russia peace deal to go through as

Russia may look to store some barrels instead of rushing to sell them".

Trading had also factored in OPEC's meeting on Sunday, which insiders has

claimed that the group would likely not change its output levels and will

potentially impose a mechanism to assess maximum output caps. Overall, it is

rather unsurprising at this point that the several monthly losses in oil

prices is largely caused by the looming prospect that the markets are

heading towards a global glut, these worries persisting regardless of the

fact that numerous analysts have challenged this view over the past few

weeks. The IEA has predicted that in 2026, global oil supply will outpace

demand by 4 million barrels per day. However, portfolio manager at

Infrastructure Capital Advisors, Andrew Meleney, has mentioned that "The

most under appreciated concept is elasticity of demand for oil: when lower

prices get passed along, we do see more consumption."

In early Asian trade this morning, crude oil prices rebounded after traders

responded to the decision made in the latest OPEC+ meeting to steadily hold

output throughout the first quarter of 2026. The group's plan to maintain

its current production levels rather than make another production hike has

brought some relief across the markets as many continue to be wary over the

prospect of a global glut. OPEC+ has reported that the market is balanced,

claiming that demand is robust enough to meet growing production, however

warnings of an oversupply amid weakening demand has dominated the sentiment

in the markets for a long time now. OPEC's offline production holds at

around 3.24 million barrels a day, with 1.24 million barrels per day making

up of voluntary cuts that been unwound this year. As multiple market

participants and analysts are expecting a global glut in 2026, driven by

rising production from OPEC+ and non-OPEC producers coupled with weakening

demand growth, has resulted in oil prices to remain far below levels earlier

this year. Despite the uptick in prices this morning, the fundamental

headwinds regarding concerns of an oversupply, which has been amplified by

prospect of sanctioned Russian barrels returning to the market after reports

that the peace deal negotiations have progressed positively so far, will

likley to bottle up sustained price rallies in the near term.

Moreover, US oil production has hit another record in September, but it

should be noted that in this month's Petroleum Supply Monthly, the report

hadn't actually included export data. The EIA had to recycle August's export

numbers and paste them into September's balance sheet due to the US Census

Bureau wasn't able to release September trade figures during a funding

lapse, making it significantly difficult for analysts to accurately read and

forecast the market, including the product supplied, implied demand, and net

imports. In simple terms, the demand figures for September is essentially a

placeholder until Census provides new data to provide an accurate

replacement. In terms of production, the figures were accurately reported:

in September US crude oil output has hit 13.84 million barrels per day,

seeing an increase of 44,000 barrels per day. Additionally, we saw record

production from New Mexico which reached 2.351 million barrels per day, and

production at the Gulf of Mexico had jumped to just under 2 million barrels

per day, the strongest rates since before the pandemic. Looking at these

figures, we can determine that longer-cycle offshore projects are doing more

of the heavy lifting as the Permian approaches its highest levels of

comfort. Notably, the rise in production has boosted worries of an over

supply whilst demand in the market is weakening, further weighing down on

market sentiment and putting additional pressure on oil prices. Until

September's disposition in data is sorted out - which the EIA as said that

it will republish September's export data once Census delivers the real

figures - the figures for supplied products will look artificially subdued,

any adjustments will look larger than they normally should, and net imports

wont provide much meaningful information. This is the worst time for the EIA

to be missing export data as market is already overwhelmed with concerns of

an oversupply. The market has been pulled in all directions: OPEC+ argues

about baselines, there's been warnings from JPMorgan, with US shale

flattening whilst the Gulf fills the gap, on top of the forecasts that

there'll be a glut by the IEA. Meanwhile, analysts will have to wait another

cycle to determine how much of that record production had actually been

exported out of the country.

IN/OUT PORT, IN VERY COLD OR VERY WARM WATER, DURING LIGHTENING OPERATION /

NATIONAL REGULATIONS IN OPERATION IN PORTS TO WHICH VESSEL IS BOUND) INCL BALLAST EXCHANGE TO BE FOR CHARTERERS' ACCOUNT. ANY SAVING IN CONSUMPTION MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY

REDUCTION IN SPEED, ANY SAVING IN TIME MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST ANY EXCESS CONSUMPTION, ANY SAVING IN FO MUST BE OFF-SET AGAINST INCREASED MDO AND VICE VERSA, AND ANY OVERALL SAVING ON INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S) MUST BE SET OFF AGAINST AN OVERALL LOSS ON OTHER INDIVIDUAL PASSAGE(S).

WRITEUP

CAPESIZE

The market closed the week on a firm and increasingly confident footing,

with the BCI 5TC rising by $2,025 on the day to finish at $37,158, up

notably from $30,685 at Mondays open. The Pacific maintained its upward

momentum, supported by two active miners and steady operator demand, with C5

fixtures ranging between $11.70 and $12.00 as the C5 index edged up to

$11.890. Conditions also improved ex-South Brazil to China, where the market

remained date sensitive, reports of $25 being fixed on C3 lifted the index

to $25.091. The Atlantic contributed further strength, with a busier North

Atlantic and a tightening of nearby positions driving meaningful gains on

fronthaul. The sharp increases in C8 and C9, up $2,062 to $43,750 and $5,500

to $61,778 respectively, underscored renewed confidence as the week drew to

a close.

Atlantic

Mittal fixed yesterday the Jera controlled Cape Kestrel (181,267 2016) for

150,000/10 Port Cartier to Kakogawa for 13-22 December at $38.50. Rio Tinto

fixed the St Shipping controlled Enna (175,975 2011) for 160,000/10 from

Seven Islands to Dhamra for 15-24 December at $34.00. Mercuria fixed a

Glencore TBN for 170,000/10 from Tubarao to Qingdao 15/19 December at

$25.00, further details have not come to light.

Asia

Mercuria fixed Golden Future (175,861 2010) for 160,000/10 from Port Hedland

to Qingdao for 14/16 December at $12.00. Rio Tinto fixed a TBN for

170,000/10 from Dampier to Qingdao for 15/17 December at $11.70, lacking

further details.

PANAMAX

The week concluded on a notably calmer note across both the Atlantic and

Asia. In the Atlantic, sentiment remained steady, with balanced fundamentals

and fronthaul demand from the U.S. Gulf and East Coast holding rates stable

rather than driving them higher. Asia showed a similarly measured pace, with

forward demand for tonnage still considered healthy. However, with time on

their side, charterers appear in no rush to commit, keeping rates broadly

unchanged. Overall, both basins reflected a quieter, more composed market

environment compared with the earlier part of the week.

Atlantic

Messrs Sail were reported to have covered their Nacala into ECI

75,000/10 coal stem on a TBN basis at $17.85 fio 33000shinc/35000shinc.

Asia

Several fixtures were reported although full details remained to be seen.

The Protector (82,506 2022) open in Makassar 6 December was said to have

fixed a trip via Koolan into China with Messrs Deyesion although a rate was

yet to surface. Multimax were also rumoured to have taken the scrubber

fitted XH Deep Cover (83,070 2024) CJK 7 December for an Australian round

voyage at $22,000 but again further clarity or details remain unknown.

SUPRAMAX

Not a great deal of surprise, the week ended on a fairly subdued note,

although the Asian arena remained in positive territory as brokers spoke of

some stronger levels being achieved albeit on a somewhat positional floor.

The Atlantic remained fairly flat as the residue of the holiday yesterday in

the United States still had an effect. That said the Mediterranean was

lacking fresh enquiry and brokers advised some owners were seeking business

elsewhere. The 11TC closed up slightly by $48 to finish the week at $18,215.

Atlantic

The Maroudio (64,723 2022) was heard to have been fixed basis delivery

Hamburg for a trip via St Petersburg to Brazil with urea at $22,000 with

Ultimar.

Asia

The Sunrise Jade (63,244 2015) Kendari 3 December was fixed for a trip to ECI with metcoke at $23,500, but no more details came to light. The Meghna

Century (66,529 2018) open Bhavnagar 3/5 December was rumoured fixed $17,500

for a trip via Arabian Gulf redelivery Bangladesh, but some heard it was

concluded on voyage basis.

HANDYSIZE

It was a typical end of weekday with little excitement emerging from the

market. The BHSI closed the day at 827 whilst the 7TC average up by $40 to

settle at $14,885. The Continent and Mediterranean had another quiet

session, with subdued activity across the board. The Aquila Trader (35,991

2012) fixed delivery Bejaia via Arzew to NC South America with steels in the

$11,000 by Swire Bulk, but further details were not surfaced. The South

Atlantic and US Gulf held steady, showing minimal fluctuation as many

awaiting for clearer direction once participants return to their desks after

the holiday period. In Asia, trading remained sluggish, with softer activity

contributing to a gentle downward trend in regional rates.

message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient

address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it.

After typing the complete address, click Send.

* Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the

address is correct.

* The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect

address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working

correctly.

More Info for Email Admins

Status code 554 5.4.14

Typically this error occurs because the recipient email address is incorrect

or doesn't exist at the destination domain. This can usually be fixed by the

sender. However, sometimes the issue needs to be fixed by the recipient or

the recipient's email admin. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above

don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one

or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address

exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are

using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is

synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as

expected. A forwarding rule can be configured by an admin via mail flow

rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the

Inbox Rules feature.

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow

settings or MX records can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow

settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up

correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records

for your domain are configured correctly.

Mail loop detected - This error also indicates that the receiving

organization's email settings are misconfigured, creating a mail loop when a

message is sent to an address that isn't found in their directory. This

usually won't disrupt mail flow for recipients that actually exist, but the

recipient's email admin should fix the misconfiguration to reduce the chance

of any other mail flow issues. A common cause for this loop is that the

recipient's domain is configured as "Internal Relay" when it should be

"Authoritative." Another common cause for the loop is that both the sender

and recipient are part of the same organization, but the sender's mailbox is

hosted by Office 365, while the recipient's mailbox is hosted on-premises,

and an outbound connector from Office 365 to the on-premises email servers

is missing or misconfigured. To fix this, the recipient's email admin should

create a correctly configured outbound connector in Office 365 to route the

message to the on-premises mailbox.

For more information and tips to fix this problem, see

<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=397497> Fix email delivery issues

for error code 5.4.14 in Office 365.
